text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Department: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 58-year-old Polish-American male with known Stage IV (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases presenting for scheduled restaging visit and urgent reassessment in the setting of markedly rising tumor markers and new radiographic findings.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski is well known to our service. He was originally diagnosed approximately 10 months ago with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma harboring a KRAS exon 2 p.G12D driver mutation (VAF 42.3%), TP53, APC, and SMAD4 co-mutations, with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases involving segments II, IV, VI, and VII. NRAS and BRAF are wild-type; tumor is MSS/MMR-proficient with low TMB (6.2 mut/Mb). Anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) is excluded based on RAS mutation status.

He received induction mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab for 12 cycles, achieving a robust partial response (approximately 58% reduction in total hepatic tumor burden, with near-complete response of the segment VII lesion). His course was complicated by cumulative Grade 3 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy after cycle 9, requiring dose reduction to 65 mg/m2 and ultimately prompting planned oxaliplatin discontinuation following completion of 12 induction cycles. He was transitioned to maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab (5 mg/kg IV every 14 days) approximately 4 months ago, consistent with an OPTIMOX1-inspired maintenance strategy.

He tolerated maintenance therapy reasonably well. His first maintenance restaging scan (approximately 8 months from diagnosis) demonstrated stable disease with minimal fluctuation in hepatic lesion size and tumor markers. CEA had declined from a peak of 187.4 ng/mL at diagnosis to a nadir of approximately 28.6 ng/mL following induction, and was 31.2 ng/mL at the 8-month scan -- reassuringly stable.

However, at his visit today, he reports approximately 3-4 weeks of new symptoms including mildly increasing exertional breathlessness -- noticeable when climbing the two flights of stairs at his school but not at rest. He also endorses worsening fatigue over the past month, now significantly limiting his after-school activities (he has had to stop his weekend walks). He has noticed his belt is tighter and reports intermittent right upper quadrant fullness after eating. He denies hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, or orthopnea. Bowel habits are unchanged -- one to two formed stools daily, no hematochezia (primary sigmoid tumor remains in place; he declined diverting colostomy at diagnosis given adequate bowel function). He has not had fevers, chills, or night sweats.

Laboratory markers drawn at the prior visit two weeks ago were alarming: CEA 94.7 ng/mL (up from 31.2 ng/mL at the 8-month scan, a tripling over approximately 6-8 weeks) and CA 19-9 287 U/mL (up from 78 U/mL). This precipitated an urgent restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast, which was performed and reviewed prior to today's visit.

The imaging findings are concerning for clear radiographic progression: the three dominant hepatic metastases (segments II, IV, VI) have all grown substantially -- the segment VI lesion is now 2.4 x 2.1 cm compared to 1.5 x 1.2 cm on the prior maintenance scan, representing roughly 60% increase in the largest dimension. The segment II and IV lesions have also grown considerably. The primary sigmoid mass has enlarged from 2.3 x 1.8 cm to 3.1 x 2.5 cm. Most significantly, three new bilateral pulmonary nodules have appeared -- an 8 mm right upper lobe nodule, a 6 mm right lower lobe nodule, and a 7 mm left upper lobe nodule -- not present on any prior imaging. There is also new small perihepatic lymphadenopathy with the largest node measuring 1.3 cm. This is unambiguous progression through maintenance fluoropyrimidine-based therapy.

This progression was discussed with the patient and his wife, who accompanied him today.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Increased fatigue (moderate, ECOG PS now 1-2 transition). No fever, chills, night sweats. Mild weight loss of approximately 3 lbs over past month.
- Respiratory: New mild exertional dyspnea as described. No hemoptysis, cough, wheezing.
- GI: Mild right upper quadrant fullness/discomfort after meals, likely related to hepatic lesion growth. Unchanged bowel function. No nausea, vomiting. No melena or hematochezia.
- Neurologic: Peripheral neuropathy has been steadily improving since oxaliplatin discontinuation. Currently Grade 1 -- mild distal fingertip paresthesias bilaterally, not functionally limiting. Gait stable. Fine motor tasks (writing on board, grading papers) back to near-normal.
- Cardiovascular: Blood pressure has been running higher at home (self-reports 155-165/95-105 mmHg range). He is on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily for bevacizumab-related hypertension.
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain.
- Genitourinary: No hematuria.
- Psychiatric: Patient understandably anxious about progression news. Denies depression. Remains engaged and motivated regarding treatment.

---

MEDICATIONS
1. Lisinopril 20 mg orally daily (hypertension -- bevacizumab-related and underlying)
2. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily (hypertension -- bevacizumab-related)
3. Atorvastatin 20 mg orally nightly (hyperlipidemia)
4. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes mellitus)
5. Duloxetine 60 mg orally daily (oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy, initiated approximately 5 months ago)
6. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours as needed (chemotherapy-induced nausea)
7. Loperamide as needed (occasional loose stools)
8. Aspirin 81 mg orally daily (cardiovascular prophylaxis, discussed with cardiology)
9. Omeprazole 20 mg orally daily (GI prophylaxis)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: BP 158/102 mmHg (right arm, seated), HR 84 bpm, RR 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, Temp 36.9°C, Weight 78.2 kg (down from 79.6 kg at last visit)
ECOG Performance Status: 1 (able to perform light work; ambulatory)

General: Alert, oriented, well-groomed. Mildly fatigued-appearing. No acute distress. Conversant and engaged throughout the visit.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Anicteric sclerae. Mucous membranes moist. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains appreciated on palpation.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. Respiratory effort non-labored at rest. No egophony.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant to deep palpation. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin (previously at the margin). No splenomegaly appreciated. No ascites by percussion. Bowel sounds present and normal. No abdominal masses palpated. Mild fullness in the left lower quadrant corresponding to the area of the sigmoid tumor, non-tender.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing or cyanosis.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 throughout. Gait steady. Bilateral distal fingertip hypoesthesia present but mild (Grade 1 neuropathy). Heel-to-shin and finger-nose intact.

Skin: No palmar-plantar erythema. No rash. Port site clean and without signs of infection.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (most recent, obtained within the past 2 weeks)
CBC: WBC 6.2 x 10^9/L, Hgb 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, likely disease-related), Hct 34.1%, Plt 289 x 10^9/L
CMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, Cl 101 mEq/L, CO2 25 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, Cr 1.02 mg/dL (eGFR 72 mL/min/1.73m2), glucose 108 mg/dL, total bili 0.9 mg/dL, direct bili 0.3 mg/dL, AST 44 U/L (mildly elevated), ALT 51 U/L (mildly elevated), Alk Phos 128 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with hepatic metastases), albumin 3.6 g/dL
Coagulation: INR 1.1, PT 12.8 sec
Tumor Markers: CEA 94.7 ng/mL (markedly elevated from 31.2 ng/mL, approximately 5 weeks prior), CA 19-9 287 U/mL (up from 78 U/mL)
HbA1c: 7.1% (adequate glycemic control on metformin)
Lipid panel: LDL 88 mg/dL (on atorvastatin, at goal)
Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 0.18 (within acceptable range; monitoring given bevacizumab use)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast (current restaging, reviewed today):

This scan was obtained urgently in the setting of rapidly rising tumor markers and new symptoms. Compared to the prior maintenance restaging CT from approximately 6-8 weeks ago:

- Liver: The hepatic metastases have grown noticeably. The segment VI lesion, which had been stable at 1.5 x 1.2 cm on the prior scan, now measures 2.4 x 2.1 cm -- roughly a 60% increase in maximum dimension. The segment II lesion is back up to 1.2 x 1.0 cm from 0.6 x 0.5 cm, essentially doubling. The segment IV lesion has grown to 1.4 x 1.1 cm from 0.7 x 0.5 cm -- also approximately doubling. The segment VII lesion, which had shown near-complete response and then disappeared on maintenance imaging, remains unvisualized. Taken together, the hepatic burden has grown significantly and rapidly.

- Lungs: Three new pulmonary nodules are present bilaterally, not seen on the prior scan. There is an 8 mm solid nodule in the right upper lobe, a 6 mm nodule in the right lower lobe, and a 7 mm nodule in the left upper lobe. These are new since the prior scan and are highly suspicious for metastatic disease in the clinical context of KRAS-mutant CRC. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

- Primary sigmoid mass: Has grown to 3.1 x 2.5 cm from 2.3 x 1.8 cm on the maintenance restaging scan -- an approximate 35% increase. The tumor remains in the sigmoid colon without evidence of frank perforation. Fat stranding around the mass is similar to prior. Pericolic lymphadenopathy is difficult to separately characterize.

- New perihepatic lymphadenopathy: A 1.3 cm portocaval/perihepatic lymph node is now visible, not definitively identified on prior scans.

- No bone lesions, adrenal masses, or peritoneal implants detected.

Overall impression: Unequivocal progression with new pulmonary metastases, hepatic lesion growth across multiple segments, and new regional lymphadenopathy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old man with KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma who has developed clear radiographic and biochemical progression through maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab. The new bilateral pulmonary nodules are the most notable development, representing likely metastatic spread to a new organ system beyond the liver. CEA has tripled to 94.7 ng/mL and CA 19-9 has risen nearly four-fold over roughly 6-8 weeks, consistent with active systemic disease acceleration.

1. DISEASE PROGRESSION -- SECOND-LINE THERAPY PLAN
The patient has progressed through first-line induction mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (12 cycles) and subsequent maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab. This is a clear failure of fluoropyrimidine-based therapy with ongoing VEGF-pathway inhibition.

Key considerations for second-line therapy selection:
- KRAS G12D mutation: Precludes use of anti-EGFR agents (cetuximab, panitumumab) outright. No KRAS G12D-targeted therapy is currently FDA-approved for colorectal cancer (sotorasib and adagrasib have very limited and underwhelming CRC response data compared to NSCLC; KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors such as MRTX1133 are in early clinical development).
- MSS/MMR-proficient: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) are not indicated; responses in MSS CRC are exceedingly rare.
- Prior bevacizumab: Per the RAISE trial, continuation of VEGF pathway inhibition via a different mechanism (VEGFR2 blockade with ramucirumab) in combination with irinotecan-based FOLFIRI demonstrated an overall survival benefit compared to FOLFIRI alone in the second-line setting. This remains a standard-of-care option.

Plan: Initiate FOLFIRI (irinotecan/leucovorin/5-FU) plus ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV on day 1 of each 2-week cycle.

UGT1A1 genotyping will be ordered prior to first irinotecan dose. If UGT1A1*28 homozygous, will start irinotecan at a reduced dose (120-150 mg/m2); if heterozygous or wild-type, will start at 150-180 mg/m2 with uptitration as tolerated. Standard antiemetic and antidiarrheal prophylaxis will be provided.

Risks discussed with patient and his wife: diarrhea (including severe Grade 3-4, especially with UGT1A1 variants), myelosuppression (particularly neutropenia), alopecia, nausea/vomiting, fatigue, mucositis. Ramucirumab-specific risks: hypertension (concerning given his current bevacizumab-related hypertension already requiring dual antihypertensives), proteinuria, wound healing impairment, arterial thromboembolic events, infusion reactions.

2. PULMONARY NODULES -- TISSUE CONFIRMATION
Given Mr. Kowalski's prior 20 pack-year smoking history (quit 8 years ago), a primary lung malignancy cannot be assumed to be excluded based on imaging morphology alone, even in the setting of known CRC metastases. The new nodules are morphologically non-specific (solid, sub-centimeter range). To avoid misattributing a primary lung tumor to metastatic CRC (with implications for staging, histology, and potentially actionable lung-specific mutations such as EGFR, ALK, ROS1), I am referring for CT-guided biopsy of the right upper lobe nodule (largest and most accessible). Interventional radiology consultation placed.

Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) also ordered to confirm persistence of KRAS G12D variant allele in circulating tumor DNA, which would further support CRC-origin for the pulmonary lesions.

3. HYPERTENSION -- ONGOING MANAGEMENT
BP today 158/102 mmHg despite lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 10 mg daily. With planned ramucirumab (VEGFR2 inhibitor), hypertension will likely worsen further -- this is a recognized class effect. Discussing with nephrology/cardiology regarding addition of a third antihypertensive agent (likely hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily or chlorthalidone) prior to ramucirumab initiation. Will check urine protein/creatinine ratio today (above). If BP not controlled to <140/90 before cycle 1, will consider brief ramucirumab hold per protocol guidelines. Ongoing co-management with internal medicine/nephrology.

4. MILD ANEMIA
Hgb 11.4 g/dL, mild. Iron studies and reticulocyte count ordered. If iron deficiency contributing, will consider IV iron supplementation (oral iron poorly tolerated with GI malignancy and prior GI symptoms). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not indicated in the context of active chemotherapy for CRC.

5. HEPATIC FUNCTION MONITORING
Mild transaminase elevation (AST 44, ALT 51) and elevated alkaline phosphatase (128 U/L) are likely related to hepatic metastases rather than medication hepatotoxicity. Will monitor closely with each cycle. Metformin continues to be appropriate at current eGFR of 72 mL/min/1.73m2; will reassess if renal function declines further (threshold for holding metformin <45 mL/min).

6. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY
Grade 1 residual oxaliplatin neuropathy, significantly improved from prior Grade 2-3. Duloxetine 60 mg daily will be continued for neuropathic symptom management and possible mood benefit. FOLFIRI does not have a neuropathy component; irinotecan's primary neurologic toxicity is cholinergic (early diarrhea, diaphoresis, lacrimation during or shortly after infusion, managed with atropine premedication).

7. KRAS G12D TARGETED THERAPY -- CLINICAL TRIAL MONITORING
Discussed the current landscape of KRAS G12D-targeted therapy in CRC. No approved KRAS G12D-specific agents exist for CRC at this time. The response rates seen with covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors (sotorasib, adagrasib) in CRC are modest (10-20%) due to upfront and acquired resistance mechanisms mediated through EGFR bypass signaling, which is particularly prominent in the CRC microenvironment. KRAS G12D inhibitors are mechanistically more challenging given the need for non-covalent binding strategies. Early-phase trials combining KRAS inhibitors with MEK inhibitors (e.g., KRAS G12D inhibitor plus binimetinib combinations) are emerging as a strategy to overcome MAPK pathway reactivation. I will maintain the patient's information with our clinical trials office. Following tissue confirmation of pulmonary metastases and after establishing stability or disease assessment on FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (typically 2-4 cycles), clinical trial enrollment may be considered for patients failing second-line therapy.

8. GOALS OF CARE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT
Had a frank and compassionate discussion with Mr. Kowalski and his wife today regarding the implications of progression. Reinforced that this remains an incurable diagnosis; however, active systemic therapy options exist and we will continue to pursue them aggressively consistent with his wishes. He remains fully engaged and motivated. He has completed an advance directive and his wife is aware of his healthcare proxy designation. He will continue to work part-time as a high school history teacher, which he finds meaningful. Referral to palliative care for symptom support (fatigue, dyspnea, psychosocial support) offered -- patient will consider. Social work referral placed.

9. FOLLOW-UP
Return visit in 2 weeks for cycle 1 FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, pending UGT1A1 genotyping results and blood pressure optimization. Tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9) to be checked at each cycle. Restaging CT planned after 4 cycles (approximately 8-10 weeks). Lung biopsy scheduled through interventional radiology within the next 7-10 days.

Time spent with patient and family: 55 minutes (>50% of time in counseling and coordination of care).

---
Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist, MD]
Department of Medical Oncology
[Institution Name]
